evidence_text: In NL2Repo-Bench, an agent receives one natural-language requirements document and an empty workspace, and must generate a fully installable Python library from scratch.
source_excerpt: “In the NL2Repo-Bench, an agent is provided with a single natural-language requirements document and an empty workspace... [and] must autonomously perform architectural design, dependency management, multi-file implementation, and packaging to produce a fully functional Python library.”
note:
This describes the benchmark task setup, not model performance.
```
caveat:
This describes the benchmark task setup, not model performance.
```

evidence_text: The benchmark does not reveal project scaffolding, source code, or test cases during development.
source_excerpt: “Crucially, no project scaffolding, source code, or test cases are revealed during development, forcing the agent to reason globally and persistently across the entire construction process.”
note:
This is a benchmark constraint on what the agent sees before evaluation.
```
caveat:
This is a benchmark constraint on what the agent sees before evaluation.
```

evidence_text: Generated repositories are evaluated by executing the original upstream pytest suite in a controlled environment.
source_excerpt: “Each generated repository is verified against the original upstream pytest suite of a real-world open-source project, executed within a controlled environment.”
note:
The paper presents this as the benchmark’s sole correctness judge.
```
caveat:
The paper presents this as the benchmark’s sole correctness judge.
```

evidence_text: The final NL2Repo-Bench dataset contains 104 tasks.
source_excerpt: “Following the rigorous selection, construction, and validation pipeline described in Section 3.1, we assemble the final NL2Repo-Bench dataset. The benchmark comprises 104 tasks...”
note:
This is the reported benchmark size after construction and validation.
```
caveat:
This is the reported benchmark size after construction and validation.
```

evidence_text: The 104 tasks are distributed across nine Python-library categories.
source_excerpt: “The benchmark comprises 104 tasks spanning nine distinct categories of Python libraries, as detailed in Table 1.”
note:
Table 1 lists the nine categories and their task counts.
```
caveat:
Table 1 lists the nine categories and their task counts.
```

evidence_text: The average NL2Repo-Bench input document length is about 18,800 tokens.
source_excerpt: “the benchmark comprises 104 tasks... with input documents averaging nearly 19k tokens” and later “The average input length of a NL2Repo-Bench task is approximately 18,800 tokens.”
note:
The source states this value in two nearby forms: “nearly 19k” and “approximately 18,800.”
```
caveat:
The source states this value in two nearby forms: “nearly 19k” and “approximately 18,800.”
```

evidence_text: Eligible target repositories had to contain between 300 and 120,000 lines of code.
source_excerpt: “Complexity. Each repository must contain 300 to 120,000 lines of code.”
note:
The paper says this range was intended to exclude trivial projects and extremely large systems.
```
caveat:
The paper says this range was intended to exclude trivial projects and extremely large systems.
```

evidence_text: Eligible target repositories had to have at least 10 GitHub stars.
source_excerpt: “Maturity. We require each repository to have at least 10 GitHub stars...”
note:
The source describes GitHub stars here as a minimal proxy for adoption, maintenance quality, and reliability.
```
caveat:
The source describes GitHub stars here as a minimal proxy for adoption, maintenance quality, and reliability.
```

evidence_text: Eligible target repositories had to include pytest-based tests, and the official repository version had to pass all of them.
source_excerpt: “Completeness and Testability. A repository must include pytest-based test cases, and its official version must successfully pass all of them.”
note:
This criterion defines benchmark eligibility, not generated-agent success.
```
caveat:
This criterion defines benchmark eligibility, not generated-agent success.
```

evidence_text: Eligible target repositories had to be created or updated within the past three years.
source_excerpt: “Recency. Only repositories that have been created or updated within the past three years are considered...”
note:
The source says this was used to reflect contemporary coding practices and dependency ecosystems.
```
caveat:
The source says this was used to reflect contemporary coding practices and dependency ecosystems.
```

evidence_text: The highest reported overall pass rate in Table 3 is 40.2% for Claude-Sonnet-4.5 using Claude Code, with Pass@1 equal to 3.
note:
This is the top entry in the reported main-results table.
```
caveat:
This is the top entry in the reported main-results table.
```

evidence_text: GPT-5 is reported at 21.7% overall pass rate with Pass@1 of 1, and Table 6 reports 78.4 average interaction turns.
note:
The source later connects GPT-5’s low turn count to premature or incomplete task completion, so these performance figures should be read alongside that caveat.
```
caveat:
The source later connects GPT-5’s low turn count to premature or incomplete task completion, so these performance figures should be read alongside that caveat.
```

evidence_text: The paper reports a GPT-5 non-finish rate of 84.5% and an early-stop rate of 13.4%.
source_excerpt: “A striking finding is the dichotomy between GPT-5’s low Early Stop rate (13.4%) and its massive Non-Finish rate (84.5%).”
note:
The source frames these rates as evidence of a “human-in-the-loop” dependency rather than a pure capability score.
```
caveat:
The source frames these rates as evidence of a “human-in-the-loop” dependency rather than a pure capability score.
```

evidence_text: Qwen3-Thinking is reported to terminate early in 49.0% of tasks and to have a non-finish rate of 46.2%.
source_excerpt: “Qwen3-Thinking terminates early in 49.0% of tasks” and Figure 5 is described as showing “an alarmingly high early stop rate of 49.0% and non-finish rate of 46.2%...”
note:
The paper presents these rates as a failure pattern, not as evidence of strong autonomous completion.
```
caveat:
The paper presents these rates as a failure pattern, not as evidence of strong autonomous completion.
```

evidence_text: Among the most frequently used tools, task_tracker has the strongest reported correlation with model performance at 0.711.
source_excerpt: “Among the three most frequently used tools, task_tracker exhibits the strongest correlation with model performance (0.711)...”
note:
This is a reported correlation, not a causal demonstration.
```
caveat:
This is a reported correlation, not a causal demonstration.
```

evidence_text: When all test cases are revealed, Claude-Sonnet-4.5 using Claude Code rises from 40.2% overall pass rate and Pass@1 of 3 to 59.4% overall pass rate and Pass@1 of 18.
source_excerpt: Table 8 reports “Document only ... Overall Avg Score (%) 40.2 Pass@1 3” and “Document + unittest ... Overall Avg Score (%) 59.4 Pass@1 18.”
note: The source explicitly states that performance remains below 60% even in this “cheating” condition, so the gain does not eliminate the benchmark difficulty.
caveat: The source explicitly states that performance remains below 60% even in this “cheating” condition, so the gain does not eliminate the benchmark difficulty.
